title: Chapter 46: Kitchen Conference
content: The jollof rice starts at 5:47 AM.

Maya knows because she's been awake since 4:30, staring at the ceiling of her childhood bedroom, listening to the sounds of her mother moving through the kitchen with the precise economy of someone who has been feeding people for thirty-seven years and sees no reason to waste a motion on sentiment.

The suitcase is open on the kitchen floor. Not the clothes side — the other side, the one that held wrapped bundles of scotch bonnet peppers and a stone mortar and a bag of crayfish that Amara had wrapped in three layers of plastic and then a towel and then a fourth layer of plastic, because TSA was not going to separate Amara Okafor from her ingredients. The woman had packed two weeks' worth of clothes in a carry-on so she could check a bag of Nigerian food.
Maya stands in the kitchen doorway and watches her mother stir.
Amara doesn't look up. "Sit down or help. Don't just stand there using air."
Maya sits.

The kitchen smells like onion and tomato and the specific sweet heat of scotch bonnets that you cannot get at the ShopRite on Flatbush. The rice isn't even in the pot yet — this is just the base, the sauce, the thing that takes an hour to become itself. Amara is stirring it with a wooden spoon that she also brought from home.
"Do we even have a pot big enough for—" Maya starts.
"I brought one."
Of course she did.

Silence settles. Not the hostile kind — they've done hostile, Maya knows that texture like she knows her own pulse. This is the loaded kind. The kind where the thing that needs to be said is so heavy it can't be lifted, so they both just sit in its shadow and pretend the shadow is normal.
Amara stirs. Maya watches.
6:02 AM. The sauce bubbles. Amara adds a pinch of something from a plastic bag she brought from Lagos.
"I looked at your website."
Maya's spine tightens. "Okay."
"The paintings are..." Amara pauses. The spoon doesn't stop moving. "Good."

From Amara, this is a standing ovation. From Amara, this is a billboard in Times Square. Maya doesn't say anything because if she says thank you it'll come out wrong, and if she says I know it'll start a fight, and if she says nothing maybe the moment will keep existing for one more second.

"The one of the capybara on the couch." Amara's voice is careful now, picking its way across the sentence like stepping stones over deep water. "The light in it. It reminded me of your father's studio."
Maya's hands go cold.
"My father—" She stops. Tries again. "Dad painted?"

Amara doesn't look up from the pot. "Before you were born. Oils. He was—" Another pause. The spoon stirs. "He was good. Not exceptional. But good. The market in Lagos, the women selling peppers. He painted the market every Saturday for two years. The light was always the same in his paintings. Soft. Like the whole place was holding its breath before the sun came fully up."

Maya's eyes fill. She's twenty-four years old and she's hearing for the first time that her father was a painter. Her father who died when she was nine. Her father whose side of the family Amara never discussed, whose memory Amara kept in a locked room in her chest and never opened for visitors.
"You never told me Dad painted."

Amara's jaw sets. The spoon doesn't slow. "He stopped when you were born. Said he had something more important to make." She looks at Maya then — actually looks, a full turn of the head, eyes dark and steady. "He was wrong. He should have kept painting. I should have made him."

Maya can count on one hand the number of times her mother has admitted being wrong about anything. She's never gotten close to this — never gotten close to the thing underneath the wrong, which isn't an apology but something more important: grief.

Amara gave Gbenga the permission to stop. She watched him set down the brushes and never said pick them back up. And now he's dead and the paintings are somewhere in a house in Lagos that nobody visits, and Amara is here in Brooklyn stirring a sauce and saying for the first and possibly only time: I should have done differently.

Maya wipes her eyes with the back of her hand. Doesn't say it's okay. It isn't okay. Doesn't say I forgive you. That's not the point. Just sits in the shadow of the thing and lets it be there between them, shared for once.

"Chloe's friend Elif," Maya says, because she doesn't know how to stay in this moment without breaking it. "Her professor at Columbia connected her with an editor. A book deal. Maybe. A collection of my paintings. Chloe's capybara. The city."
"How much?" Amara asks immediately, because Amara.
"Maybe sixty thousand over two years."
Amara makes a sound — not quite dismissive, not quite impressed. "That's not enough."
"It's enough for now."

The phrase lands in the kitchen like a coin dropping into still water. Rippling outward. Amara looks at her daughter — really looks, the way she looked at the website, the way she looked at the pot of sauce to see if it was ready. She sees something she didn't expect.
Maya has learned something Amara never could.

Enough for now. Not enough forever. Not enough to be safe, to be certain, to wrap in three layers of plastic and a towel and a fourth layer of plastic and carry across an ocean. Just enough for now. Enough to keep going, to keep making, to keep the light in the painting soft and the breath held.

Amara never learned "enough for now." She learned "never enough" and built a whole life around that architecture — the savings, the overtime, the two jobs, the suitcase full of peppers because who knew when she'd get back to Lagos and need them again. Amara's whole life was preparing for the moment when there wouldn't be enough, and here is her daughter, who has almost nothing, saying it's enough for now like it's the most natural thing in the world.
It is the most natural thing in the world. Amara just forgot.
She turns back to the pot. Adds the rice. The water hisses.
"The light in your painting," Amara says, not looking up. "It was the same as his. The way the couch caught the sun. Before it was fully up."
Maya's throat closes. She nods even though her mother can't see her.
The jollof rice cooks. The kitchen fills with the smell of home — Lagos and Brooklyn at the same time, the way light is the same everywhere, the way soft is the same in every language.
At 6:41 AM, Amara tastes the rice and pronounces it acceptable.
It's perfect.
Maya doesn't say so. Some things you don't need to say. You just sit in the kitchen with them and let them be true.
